name: digital-access-programs
description: Free and subsidized internet, devices, digital literacy programs, and tech resources for low-income individuals and students. Directly tied to educational access.
source: Liberation Labs mutual aid knowledge base

triples.subject: Affordable Connectivity Program (ACP)
triples.object: up to $30/month internet subsidy ($75 on tribal lands) for qualifying households — check current program status as funding has been intermittent

triples.subject: Lifeline program
triples.object: $9.25/month discount on phone or internet service for households at or below 135% FPL or participating in SNAP, Medicaid, SSI, etc.

triples.subject: public library
triples.object: free computer access, WiFi, digital literacy classes, and often mobile hotspot lending — no income verification required

triples.subject: Pell Grant recipient
triples.object: various device lending and internet discount programs — Pell eligibility is a common qualifying criterion

triples.subject: refurbished laptop program
triples.object: income verification or enrollment proof — organizations like PCs for People, Human-I-T, and Digitunity provide devices at low or no cost

triples.subject: school district
triples.object: 1:1 device programs (Chromebook/laptop) and home internet hotspots for enrolled students — ask the school technology coordinator

triples.subject: FCC Emergency Broadband Benefit
triples.object: ACP — check current program status; successor programs may exist

triples.subject: digital literacy training
triples.object: public libraries, community colleges, workforce development programs, and senior centers — often free

triples.subject: low-cost internet plan
triples.object: most major ISPs for qualifying households — typically $10-30/month for basic broadband, ask about income-qualified tiers

triples.subject: mobile phone
triples.object: Lifeline-participating carriers who provide free smartphones with monthly data plans to qualifying individuals

triples.subject: assistive technology
triples.object: state Assistive Technology Programs (every state has one) — screen readers, adaptive keyboards, magnification software at no cost

triples.subject: workforce training program
triples.object: digital skills training, device access, and internet stipends as part of the program — ask during enrollment

triples.subject: community technology center
triples.object: free computer access, classes, and tech support in underserved neighborhoods — often in community centers, housing authorities, or faith organizations

triples.subject: tribal broadband
triples.object: enhanced ACP benefit ($75/month), tribal library programs, and USDA ReConnect grants for rural tribal areas

triples.subject: veterans
triples.object: VA digital divide programs, veteran-serving organizations with device programs, and GI Bill technology allowances

triples.subject: telehealth access
triples.object: reliable internet and a device with camera/microphone — FQHCs (community health centers) often help patients get set up

triples.subject: online safety
triples.object: strong unique passwords (use a password manager), two-factor authentication, recognizing phishing, and keeping software updated

triples.subject: digital access barrier
triples.object: benefits applications, reasonable accommodation requests, and school IEP/504 plans — it's a recognized barrier that triggers support obligations

triples.subject: hotspot lending
triples.object: many public libraries for 2-4 week checkout periods — provides home internet access without a subscription

triples.subject: E-Rate program
triples.object: discounted internet and telecommunications to schools and libraries — schools can use this to fund student home internet programs
